front.title: Netcapstring: A Self-Describing Capacity-Aware Byte Frame

abstract.t: This document defines the netcapstring, a self-describing capacity-aware binary frame for encoding byte sequences. A netcapstring extends the netstring concept with a separate capacity field, allowing data to grow in place without shifting subsequent entries in a stream or file. The format is suitable for memory-mapped storage, append-oriented logs, and any context where in-place updates of variable-length records are desirable.

The encoding is pure ASCII for the header and newline-terminated, making it easy to inspect with standard tools while remaining efficient for machine parsing. No escaping is required: the data region may contain arbitrary bytes, including newlines, colons, and null bytes.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Netstrings, as described by D. J. Bernstein, encode a byte sequence as its decimal length, a colon, the raw bytes, and a trailing comma. For example, the string "hello" is encoded as "5:hello,". This format is simple, unambiguous, and immune to delimiter-in-data problems.

However, netstrings offer no mechanism for in-place updates. If the data grows by even one byte, every subsequent byte in the stream must shift to accommodate the new length. In storage systems where entries are written sequentially and may be updated later, this limitation forces either full rewrites or complex indirection layers.
This document defines the netcapstring ("net capacity string"), which extends netstrings with two additions:

ol.li: A capacity field that reserves space beyond the current data length, allowing the data to grow in place up to the reserved capacity without affecting neighboring entries.
A number field that describes the width of both the capacity and used fields, making the header self-describing without requiring magic constants or fixed-width assumptions.

section.t: These additions preserve the netstring virtues (simplicity, no escaping, unambiguous framing) while enabling a new class of use cases: memory-mapped blob stores, configuration databases with in-place edits, and any system that benefits from pre-allocated growth room in serialized records.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Overview
section.t: A netcapstring encodes a byte sequence with reserved capacity for future growth. The general format is:
When the data fills the capacity exactly (cap equals used), there is no padding:
The header (number, cap, used) occupies its own line, terminated by a newline (0x0A). The data region follows on the next line, space-padded to the full capacity, and is also terminated by a newline.

section.name: Field Definitions
section.t: A netcapstring consists of the following fields, in order:

tr.td: Digit count of cap (decimal ASCII, no leading zeros)
Capacity in bytes (decimal ASCII, zero-padded to number digits)
Used bytes (decimal ASCII, zero-padded to number digits)

section.name: The Number Field
section.t: The number field is the decimal representation of the number of digits in the cap field. It MUST NOT have leading zeros. It MUST be a positive integer.

The number field exists because both cap and used are zero-padded to a fixed width. Fixed-width fields are essential for in-place updates: when data grows, only the used field changes, and its byte width must remain constant so the header size is preserved. But the parser cannot know that width without being told. A netstring avoids this problem because it has only one length field, which the parser reads by scanning digits until a colon. With two fixed-width numeric fields, the number field tells the parser how many bytes to read for each.

This design also improves parsing efficiency. A netstring parser must read one byte at a time, checking each for a colon, until the full length is collected. A netcapstring parser reads one byte (the number field is almost always a single digit), computes the total header size from that value, and then reads the entire header in a single operation. The full sequence is three reads: read(1) for the number field, read(header_size) for the rest of the header, and read(cap) for the data and padding. While operating systems typically cache byte-at-a-time reads, the ability to issue fixed-size reads makes the parsing model simpler and more predictable.
Implementations SHOULD reject number values larger than 19, which covers the full range of 64-bit integers.

section.name: Capacity and Used Fields
section.t: The cap field is the total capacity reserved for the data region, in bytes, encoded as a decimal integer and zero-padded to exactly number digits. The used field is the number of bytes currently occupied by data, encoded identically.
The following invariants MUST hold:

ul.li: cap MUST be greater than or equal to zero.
used MUST be greater than or equal to zero.
used MUST be less than or equal to cap.

section.t: Because used is always zero-padded to the same width as cap, and used is always less than or equal to cap, the header size never changes when data grows within the reserved capacity. This is the critical invariant for in-place updates: the header is a fixed-size buffer that can be overwritten in place when the used count changes. A writer updates the used field and writes new data without modifying the header length or shifting any bytes.

section.name: Data Region
section.t: The data region contains exactly used bytes of arbitrary content. There are no restrictions on the byte values within the data region: it MAY contain newlines (0x0A), colons (0x3A), commas (0x2C), null bytes (0x00), or any other octet. No escaping is performed.

When cap is greater than used, the remaining cap - used bytes are filled with spaces (0x20). There is no data-end marker: the used field already encodes the data length, so no in-band delimiter is needed. This design choice has three benefits: it simplifies the padding size calculation (always cap - used, no off-by-one for a marker byte), it means the data can always be copied verbatim from the raw file without stripping a trailing delimiter, and it eliminates the ambiguity that arises when cap equals used and the marker and terminator would occupy the same byte position.
When cap equals used, there is no padding. The terminator immediately follows the last data byte.

section.name: Terminator
section.t: The final byte of a netcapstring is always a newline (0x0A). There are no exceptions: standalone entries, entries within a sequence, and containers all use the same terminator. This uniformity means the format has exactly one framing rule regardless of nesting depth.

section.name: Human Readability
section.t: The choice of newline (0x0A) as terminator and space (0x20) as padding byte is deliberate: it makes netcapstring-encoded data inspectable with standard text tools. Because each entry ends with a newline, utilities such as cat, head, tail, less, and wc -l produce useful output on raw netcapstring files. The ASCII header (decimal digits and colons) is immediately readable, and the space-filled padding appears as blank space rather than binary noise.

This is a departure from the original netstring design, which uses a comma as the sole delimiter. The newline terminator and space padding mean that netcapstring-encoded data can be inspected naturally with any text tool, without requiring specialized utilities or hex editors.

section.name: Header Length Computation
section.t: The total header length for a netcapstring with a given capacity is deterministic:
Where digit_count(n) returns the number of decimal digits needed to represent the non-negative integer n (with digit_count(0) = 1).
The total on-disk size of a netcapstring is header_len + cap + 1 (the +1 accounts for the terminator byte).

section.name: Examples
section.t: The following examples illustrate netcapstring encoding. Spaces in the padding region are shown as dots for clarity.

section.name: Data with Padding
section.t: The string "hello" (5 bytes) with capacity 16:
section.artwork:
2:16:05
 hello...........

number = 2 (cap has 2 digits) cap = 16 (zero-padded to 2 digits) used = 05 (zero-padded to 2 digits) 
 = header terminator data = "hello" (5 bytes) pad = 11 space bytes 
 = data terminator

section.name: Empty Data
section.t: Empty data with capacity 8:
section.artwork:
1:8:0
 ........

number = 1 cap = 8 used = 0 
 = header terminator data = (empty) pad = 8 space bytes 
 = data terminator

section.name: Exact Fit
section.t: The string "abcd" (4 bytes) with capacity 4:
section.artwork:
1:4:4
 abcd

number = 1 cap = 4 used = 4 
 = header terminator data = "abcd" (4 bytes) 
 = data terminator (no padding)
section.t: Since cap equals used, there is no padding. The newline terminator immediately follows the data.

section.name: Large Capacity
section.t: The string "x" (1 byte) with capacity 100:
section.artwork:
3:100:001
 x<99 spaces>

number = 3 (cap has 3 digits) cap = 100 (zero-padded to 3 digits) used = 001 (zero-padded to 3 digits) 
 = header terminator data = "x" (1 byte) pad = 99 space bytes 
 = data terminator

section.name: Parsing Algorithm
section.t: A conforming parser MUST implement the following steps to decode a netcapstring from a byte buffer at a given offset:

ol.li: Scan forward from the current offset, collecting ASCII digit bytes (0x30-0x39) until a colon (0x3A) is encountered. Interpret the collected digits as the decimal integer N (the number field). If N is zero, negative, or exceeds the implementation's limit, the input is malformed.
Read the next N bytes and interpret them as the decimal integer cap (the capacity). If fewer than N bytes remain, the input is truncated.
Verify the next byte is a colon (0x3A). Advance past it.
Read the next N bytes and interpret them as the decimal integer used. If fewer than N bytes remain, the input is truncated.
Verify the next byte is a newline (0x0A). This is the header terminator. Advance past it.
Validate: cap MUST be non-negative, used MUST be non-negative, used MUST NOT exceed cap.

Verify that at least cap + 1 bytes remain in the buffer (the data/padding region plus the terminator). This check MUST use subtraction rather than addition to avoid integer overflow on crafted inputs.
Read used bytes of data from the current position.
Skip cap - used bytes of space padding.
Verify the byte at offset + cap is a newline (0x0A). This is the terminator.
The next netcapstring (if any) begins at offset + cap + 1.

section.t: A parser MUST NOT assume that padding bytes are spaces. While conforming encoders fill padding with spaces, a parser receiving data from an untrusted source SHOULD treat the padding region as opaque and skip it based on the cap and used values alone.

section.name: In-Place Updates
section.t: The primary motivation for netcapstrings is enabling in-place updates. When data within a netcapstring grows but remains within the reserved capacity, the update procedure is:

ol.li: Write the new data bytes starting at the data region offset.
Space-fill the remaining capacity after the new data.
Update the used field in the header with the new length, zero-padded to the same width. The header size does not change because used is always zero-padded to number digits and used never exceeds cap.

section.t: No other bytes in the buffer need to move. This property makes netcapstrings particularly suited for memory-mapped files where random writes are efficient but insertions are expensive.

section.name: Capacity Growth Policy
section.t: When data outgrows its capacity, the netcapstring must be rewritten with a larger capacity. This document does not mandate a specific growth policy; implementations MAY choose any strategy. A common approach is to set the new capacity to the data length plus 10%, providing room for incremental growth while avoiding excessive waste.

Note that if the new capacity requires more digits than the old capacity (e.g., growing from cap=99 to cap=100), the header width changes and the entry must be relocated. Implementations SHOULD account for this in their growth policy to minimize relocations.

section.name: Concatenation and Streams
section.t: Multiple netcapstrings MAY be concatenated in a byte stream. Each netcapstring is self-delimiting: a parser decodes one entry, advances to the offset returned by the parsing algorithm, and begins decoding the next. No additional framing or separators are needed between entries.

A netcapstring whose data region contains other netcapstrings is called a container. Containers enable hierarchical structures (e.g., a file format containing multiple named entries). The container's capacity encompasses all nested entries plus any reserved space for future entries.

Applications using containers SHOULD define a sentinel byte to mark the end of the entry list within the container's data region (e.g., a newline byte), so that parsers can distinguish active entries from reserved padding space.

section.name: Comparison with Netstrings

tr.td: No (length field is variable-width but must be fully scanned)
Yes (number field tells parser how wide cap and used are)

section.t: The additional header overhead (typically 4-6 extra bytes for entries under 10,000 bytes) is negligible for most applications and is the cost of enabling in-place updates.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Implementations parsing netcapstrings from untrusted sources MUST guard against the following:

ul.li: Integer overflow: The cap field may contain very large values. The buffer-remaining check (step 7 of the parsing algorithm) MUST use subtraction (cap + 1 > len(buf) - off) rather than addition (off + cap + 1 > len(buf)) to avoid integer overflow on crafted inputs where cap approaches the maximum integer value.

Resource exhaustion: A crafted number field could claim an unreasonably wide cap field. Implementations SHOULD impose a maximum on the number field (19 digits covers 64-bit integers) and reject inputs exceeding it.

Memory allocation: Implementations that allocate buffers based on the cap value MUST validate that the cap value is consistent with the actual remaining input before allocating. A small input claiming a multi-gigabyte capacity MUST be rejected.

section.t: The data region is opaque; the format provides no integrity protection. Applications requiring data authentication SHOULD layer a MAC or digital signature over the serialized output.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.name: Acknowledgements
section.t: The netcapstring format is directly inspired by D. J. Bernstein's netstrings . The core insight of using a length-prefixed, delimiter-terminated frame for arbitrary data originates from that work. The capacity extension was motivated by the needs of the ZeFS blob store, where configuration entries must support in-place updates in memory-mapped files.
